stem: The term 'positive symptoms' in schizophrenia describes symptoms that are additional or excess experiences not typically present in healthy individuals. Which of the following lists ONLY positive symptoms of schizophrenia?
options: Hallucination, Delusion, Thought disorders, and Illusion
Avolition, Alogia, Anhedonia, and Blunt affect
Associations loosening, Mannerism, Disorganized speech, and Echopraxia

stem: The term 'negative symptoms' in schizophrenia describes a specific category of symptoms involving reduction or absence of normal functions. Which of the following lists ONLY negative symptoms of schizophrenia?
options: Avolition, Alogia, Blunt/Flattened Affect, Anhedonia, Apathy, and Inattention
Hallucination, Delusion, Thought disorders, and Illusion
Associations loosening, Mannerism, Disorganized speech, Echopraxia, Stereotyping, and Catatonic stupor

stem: Disorganized symptoms of schizophrenia are described as a third category. Which of the following lists ONLY disorganized symptoms as numbered in the text?
options: Associations loosening/derailment, Mannerism, Disorganized speech, Echopraxia, Stereotyping, Negativism, and Catatonic stupor and excitement
Avolition, Alogia, Blunt affect, Anhedonia, Apathy, and Inattention
Hallucination, Delusion, Thought disorders, Illusion, Insomnia, and Lack of insight

stem: Eugene Bleuler maintained the 4 A's as distinctive and fundamental features of schizophrenia. Which combination CORRECTLY identifies ALL four of Bleuler's A's?
options: Affect, Autism, Ambivalence, and Association loosening
Affect, Apathy, Avolition, and Alogia
Affect, Autism, Anhedonia, and Ambivalence

stem: Bleuler's 'Autism' as a feature of schizophrenia refers specifically to which of the following?
options: Socially withdrawn, preferring to live in a world of fantasy rather than interacting with the social world â the intent of autism by Eugene was to catch the behavioural and social impediment of schizophrenia
The existence of contradictory emotional feelings towards an object, commonly of love and hate toward the same object or person â there is conflict and ambiguities
Inappropriate or flattened affect that is incongruent to the circumstances â a major core part of schizophrenia where there is lack of normal range of emotion

stem: Bleuler's 'Ambivalence' as a feature of schizophrenia is defined in the text as which of the following?
options: The existence of contradictory emotional feelings towards an object, commonly of love and hate toward the same object or person; conflicting attitude and emotion towards others and self; conflict and ambiguities regarding what one desires for self and for others; lack of motivation and depersonalization
Socially withdrawn, preferring to live in a world of fantasy rather than interacting with the social world â catching the behavioural and social impediment of schizophrenia
Inappropriate or flattened affect incongruent to circumstances; lack of normal range of emotion; maintaining a flat appearance without smiling, frowning, or making any emotional expression

stem: DÃ©jÃ  vu and Jamais vu are listed as symptoms of schizophrenia. Which pair CORRECTLY contrasts their definitions?
options: DÃ©jÃ  vu: when a person feels undue familiarity to an event or person; Jamais vu: a feeling that a familiar situation is strange
DÃ©jÃ  vu: a feeling that a familiar situation is strange; Jamais vu: when a person feels undue familiarity to an event or person
DÃ©jÃ  vu: the invention of new meaningless words; Jamais vu: monotonous repetition of phrases

stem: Paranoid Type schizophrenia is described in the text. Which statement MOST accurately characterises this subtype?
options: Characterized by prominent delusions and auditory hallucinations; individuals had persecutory or grandiose delusions, believing that others were plotting against them or that they possessed special powers or abilities
Characterized by disorganized speech, behaviour, and affect; individuals exhibited incoherent or illogical speech, inappropriate emotional responses, and disorganized or bizarre behaviour
Characterized by disturbances in motor behaviour; individuals may display extreme immobility or rigidity (catatonic stupor) or excessive and purposeless motor activity (catatonic excitement)

stem: Simple Schizophrenia is described in the text as occurring at which developmental stage, and what are its hallmark features?
options: Shows a progressive or insidious mental deterioration occurring at the time of puberty with increased apathy, lack of initiative, while there is gradual change in the individual's personality, inability to meet the demands of society, and decline in total performance

Involves disturbances in motor behaviour with extreme immobility or rigidity (catatonic stupor) or excessive and purposeless motor activity (catatonic excitement), along with mutism, echolalia, and echopraxia
Characterized by persistent mild or attenuated symptoms indicating a period of partial or incomplete remission with lingering negative symptoms or attenuated positive symptoms

stem: Schizoaffective Schizophrenia is described as manifesting with features of two different conditions. Which two conditions are combined in schizoaffective schizophrenia?
options: Schizophrenia and affective/mood disorder (depression and mania)
Schizophrenia and obsessive-compulsive disorder
Schizophrenia and anxiety disorder

stem: Catatonic Schizophrenia involves disturbances in motor behaviour. Which term in the text specifically describes the state where the patient assumes a strange and bizarre posture for a long time?
options: Catatonic stupor â where the patient assumes a strange and bizarre posture for a long time, also called Waxy flexibility
Catatonic excitement â excessive and purposeless motor activity
Echopraxia â imitating others' movements automatically in catatonic schizophrenia

stem: The Residual Type of schizophrenia is distinguished from the Undifferentiated Type. Which pair CORRECTLY contrasts these two subtypes?
options: Residual Type: person had at least one episode of schizophrenia but currently only displays mild or attenuated symptoms â a period of partial or incomplete remission with lingering negative symptoms; Undifferentiated Type: person exhibits symptoms of schizophrenia but does not fit into any specific subtype

Residual Type: person exhibits symptoms of schizophrenia but does not fit into any specific subtype; Undifferentiated Type: person had at least one episode of schizophrenia but currently only displays mild or attenuated symptoms
Residual Type: characterized by disorganized speech and bizarre behaviour with no identifiable subtype features; Undifferentiated Type: person has had a period of partial or incomplete remission

stem: According to DSM-5 Characteristic Symptoms, a diagnosis of schizophrenia requires the presence of two or more of five specific symptoms. Which option lists ALL five characteristic symptoms?
options: Delusions, Hallucinations, Disorganized speech, Grossly disorganized or catatonic behaviour, and Negative symptoms (e.g., diminished emotional expression, avolition)
Delusions, Hallucinations, Disorganized speech, Blunt affect, and Alogia
Hallucinations, Delusions, Thought disorders, Illusion, and Catatonic stupor

stem: The DSM-5 Duration criterion for schizophrenia specifies that continuous signs of illness must be present for at least how long, and the active phase must last at least how long?
options: Continuous signs for at least six months, including at least one month of active-phase symptoms (or less if successfully treated) and may include periods of prodromal or residual symptoms
Continuous signs for at least three months, including at least two weeks of active-phase symptoms and may include periods of prodromal or residual symptoms
Continuous signs for at least twelve months, including at least six months of active-phase symptoms and may include periods of prodromal or residual symptoms

stem: Good Prognostic Factors for schizophrenia are listed in the text. Which of the following is listed as a GOOD prognostic factor?
options: Late onset, female sex, married marital status, short duration, family history of mood disorder, paranoid and catatonic subtype, good premorbid personality, predominance of positive symptoms, and good social support
Insidious/early onset, male sex, single/divorced/widowed status, long duration, family history of schizophrenia, predominance of negative symptoms, and poor social support
Absence of precipitating factor, poor premorbid personality, predominance of negative symptoms, and simple and undifferentiated subtype

stem: Poor Prognostic Factors for schizophrenia are listed in the text. Which of the following is listed as a POOR prognostic factor?
options: Insidious/early onset, male sex, single/divorced/widowed marital status, long duration, family history of schizophrenia especially simple and undifferentiated subtype, absence of precipitating factor, poor premorbid personality, predominance of negative symptoms, and poor social support
Late onset, female sex, married status, short duration, family history of mood disorder, paranoid and catatonic subtype, good premorbid personality, and good social support
Paranoid subtype, good social support, late onset, predominance of positive symptoms, and good premorbid personality

stem: Typical antipsychotic drugs used in the management of schizophrenia are named in the text. Which group CORRECTLY lists ONLY typical antipsychotics as mentioned?
options: Chlorpromazine, Haloperidol, Thioridazine, and Trifluoperazine
Risperidone, Clozapine, and Olanzepine
Chlorpromazine, Haloperidol, Risperidone, and Clozapine

stem: Atypical antipsychotic drugs used in schizophrenia management are named in the text. Which group CORRECTLY lists ONLY atypical antipsychotics as mentioned?
options: Risperidone, Clozapine, and Olanzepine
Chlorpromazine, Haloperidol, Thioridazine, and Trifluoperazine
Haloperidol, Risperidone, and Chlorpromazine

stem: Social Skills Training is listed as a management strategy for schizophrenia. Which specific behaviours does it address?
options: Helps to address behaviours such as poor eye contact, odd facial expression, and lack of spontaneity in social situations through role play and homework assignments
Helps to reduce the frequency of bizarre, disturbing, and deviant behaviour through applied behaviour analysis and contingency management
Promotes social interaction, sense of cohesiveness, identification, and insight among multiple patients in a group setting

stem: Behavioural Therapy, Group Therapy, and Family Therapy are all listed as management strategies for schizophrenia. Which pair CORRECTLY distinguishes the purposes of Behavioural Therapy versus Family Therapy?
options: Behavioural Therapy: to reduce the frequency of bizarre, disturbing, and deviant behaviour; Family Therapy: to educate the family about schizophrenia, reduce tension, involve the family, and prevent relapse
Behavioural Therapy: to educate the family about schizophrenia and prevent relapse; Family Therapy: to reduce the frequency of bizarre, disturbing, and deviant behaviour
Behavioural Therapy: to promote social interaction, sense of cohesiveness, identification, and insight; Family Therapy: to address poor eye contact and odd facial expression through role play

stem: Rehabilitation is listed as a management strategy for schizophrenia. The text identifies several activities under Rehabilitation. Which of the following is CORRECTLY listed?
options: To reduce residual effects of mental illness on the patient; activities to develop work habits, training new vocation, retraining in a previous skill, vocational guidance, and independent job placement
To promote social interaction, sense of cohesiveness, identification, and insight among multiple patients in a therapeutic setting
To make the patient independent and useful to self and the society through occupational activities

stem: PTSD is defined in the text as a mental health condition that can develop in individuals who have experienced or witnessed a traumatic event. The traumatic event must be one that involves which specific elements?
options: Actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence â which triggers a range of distressing symptoms that may persist for months or even years after the event
Any emotionally distressing experience such as relationship breakdown, job loss, or academic failure that significantly impacts the individual's daily functioning
Any experience that causes persistent guilt, shame, or regret, regardless of whether physical harm was involved or threatened

stem: PTSD symptoms are categorised into four main clusters. Which option CORRECTLY identifies ALL FOUR symptom clusters?
options: Re-experiencing symptoms, Avoidance symptoms, Hyperarousal symptoms, and Cognitive and mood symptoms
Re-experiencing symptoms, Avoidance symptoms, Hyperarousal symptoms, and Dissociative symptoms
Intrusive memories, Emotional numbing, Hypervigilance, and Social withdrawal

stem: Hyperarousal symptoms of PTSD are described in the text. Which of the following MOST accurately describes this cluster?
options: Includes symptoms such as hypervigilance, exaggerated startle response, difficulty concentrating, irritability, and sleep disturbances; individuals may be constantly on edge and have an increased sensitivity to potential threats
Includes intrusive memories, flashbacks, and nightmares related to the traumatic event; these experiences are distressing and cause intense emotional and physical reactions
Includes persistent feelings of guilt, shame, anger, or emotional numbness; the individual develops negative thoughts or beliefs about themselves, others, or the world

stem: The psychotherapy approaches specifically named in the text for PTSD management include which of the following?
options: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT), Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR), and Prolonged Exposure Therapy (PE)
Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT), Motivational Interviewing (MI), and Contingency Management
Psychodynamic therapy, Dialectical Behaviour Therapy (DBT), and Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT)

stem: Medications named in the text for managing PTSD symptoms, particularly when associated anxiety or depressive symptoms are present, include which of the following?
options: Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs) and Serotonin-Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitors (SNRIs)
Typical antipsychotics such as Chlorpromazine and Haloperidol
Benzodiazepines and mood stabilizers such as Lithium carbonate

stem: Dementia is defined in the text. Which statement MOST accurately reflects the definition of dementia?
options: A syndrome characterized by a progressive and persistent decline in cognitive abilities including memory, thinking, reasoning, and problem-solving skills; it is not a specific disease but rather a term used to describe a group of symptoms associated with a loss of cognitive function
A specific disease characterized by the accumulation of abnormal protein plaques (beta-amyloid) and tangles (tau) in the brain, leading to progressive loss of brain cells and cognitive decline
A psychiatric disorder characterized by acute onset of confusion, disorientation, and cognitive impairment, typically reversible with treatment of the underlying medical cause

stem: Alzheimer's disease is described as the most common form of dementia. Which of the following MOST accurately describes its pathological mechanism?
options: Characterized by the accumulation of abnormal protein plaques (beta-amyloid) and tangles (tau) in the brain, leading to progressive loss of brain cells and cognitive decline
Occurs when there is damage to the blood vessels in the brain leading to reduced blood flow and oxygen supply; can result from stroke, small vessel disease, or other vascular disorders
Characterized by the presence of abnormal protein deposits called Lewy bodies in the brain; individuals may experience cognitive fluctuations, visual hallucinations, and Parkinson's-like symptoms

stem: Lewy Body Dementia (LBD) is described in the text. Which constellation of features MOST specifically characterises LBD?
options: Presence of abnormal protein deposits called Lewy bodies; individuals experience cognitive fluctuations, visual hallucinations, Parkinson's-like symptoms such as tremors and stiffness, and fluctuations in alertness and attention
Damage to blood vessels in the brain leading to reduced blood flow and oxygen supply; can result from stroke, small vessel disease, or other vascular disorders
Primarily affects the frontal and temporal lobes; causes changes in behaviour, personality, language difficulties, and problems with executive functioning

stem: Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD) is described in the text. Which statement MOST accurately describes FTD?
options: A group of dementias that primarily affect the frontal and temporal lobes of the brain; can cause changes in behaviour, personality, language difficulties, and problems with executive functioning
A dementia characterized by the presence of abnormal protein deposits called Lewy bodies; individuals experience cognitive fluctuations, visual hallucinations, and Parkinson's-like symptoms
The most common form of dementia accounting for a significant majority of cases; characterized by accumulation of beta-amyloid plaques and tau tangles leading to progressive cognitive decline

stem: Parkinson's Disease Dementia (PDD) is described in the text. Which statement MOST accurately describes PDD?
options: Parkinson's disease is a movement disorder that can progress to include cognitive impairment and dementia; people with PDD often experience motor symptoms such as tremors, rigidity, and bradykinesia in addition to cognitive decline
PDD primarily affects the frontal and temporal lobes causing changes in behaviour, personality, language difficulties, and problems with executive functioning before motor symptoms appear
PDD is characterized by visual hallucinations and cognitive fluctuations as the primary features, with motor symptoms developing late in the disease course

stem: Vascular Dementia is described in the text. Which statement MOST accurately describes its mechanism and causes?
options: Occurs when there is damage to the blood vessels in the brain leading to reduced blood flow and oxygen supply; can result from stroke, small vessel disease, or other vascular disorders
Characterized by the accumulation of abnormal protein plaques (beta-amyloid) and tangles (tau) in the brain, leading to progressive loss of brain cells and cognitive decline
Characterized by the presence of Lewy bodies; individuals experience cognitive fluctuations, visual hallucinations, and Parkinson's-like symptoms

stem: Association loosening in schizophrenia is described in the text. What does the cognitive underpinning of association loosening lead to?
options: The disorganization/loosening of thought association leads to word salad, flight of ideas, and thought disorder
The disorganization of association leads to confabulation where the patient fills memory gaps with imaginary events
The loosening of thought association leads to circumstantiality where the patient uses excessive trivial details before reaching the central point

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 26-year-old man is admitted with a 7-month history of believing that his neighbours are monitoring his home with hidden cameras and that the government has implanted a device in his brain to control his thoughts. He hears voices confirming his suspicions. He has never had a full manic episode. Which subtype of schizophrenia (pre-DSM-5) does this presentation MOST suggest?
options: Paranoid Type â characterized by prominent delusions and auditory hallucinations; individuals had persecutory or grandiose delusions, believing others were plotting against them or that they possessed special powers
Catatonic Type â involving disturbances in motor behaviour including extreme immobility or rigidity and excessive purposeless motor activity
Undifferentiated Type â symptoms of schizophrenia that do not fit into any specific subtype but are significant enough to meet the criteria for a diagnosis of schizophrenia

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A psychiatric nurse is assessing a patient with schizophrenia. During the interview, the patient maintains a fixed, rigid posture with one arm raised for 45 minutes and does not speak. Which clinical feature is MOST evident?
options: Catatonic stupor â extreme immobility or rigidity (waxy flexibility) where the patient assumes a strange and bizarre posture for a long time
Catatonic excitement â excessive and purposeless motor activity characteristic of catatonic schizophrenia
Psychomotor retardation â slowing down of bodily movement combined with slowing of thought, more common in depressive states

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 35-year-old female veteran returns from a conflict zone where she witnessed multiple deaths. She now has nightmares every night, avoids watching the news or anything that reminds her of the war, is constantly scanning her environment for threats, and believes she is permanently damaged. Which cluster of PTSD symptoms does her belief that she is 'permanently damaged' MOST represent?
options: Cognitive and mood symptoms â PTSD can affect a person's thinking patterns and emotions; she may develop negative thoughts or beliefs about herself, others, or the world; persistent feelings of guilt, shame, anger, or emotional numbness
Hyperarousal symptoms â including hypervigilance, exaggerated startle response, difficulty concentrating, irritability, and sleep disturbances; she is constantly on edge
Avoidance symptoms â actively avoiding places, people, activities, or situations that remind her of the traumatic event

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 75-year-old man is brought to clinic by his wife. She reports he has been having vivid visual hallucinations of small animals, his alertness fluctuates significantly throughout the day, and he has developed a shuffling gait and tremors. His cognitive decline began after the motor symptoms. Which type of dementia is MOST consistent?
options: Lewy Body Dementia (LBD) â characterized by Lewy bodies; individuals experience cognitive fluctuations, visual hallucinations, Parkinson's-like symptoms such as tremors and stiffness, and fluctuations in alertness and attention
Alzheimer's Disease â characterized by accumulation of beta-amyloid plaques and tau tangles leading to progressive loss of brain cells and cognitive decline
Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD) â primarily affecting the frontal and temporal lobes causing changes in behaviour, personality, language difficulties, and problems with executive functioning

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nurse caring for a patient with schizophrenia notices she consistently avoids eye contact, rarely initiates conversation, and has an odd facial expression that makes social interaction very difficult. Which management strategy is MOST specifically indicated for this presentation?
options: Social Skills Training â helps to address behaviours such as poor eye contact, odd facial expression, and lack of spontaneity in social situations through role play and homework assignments
Behavioural Therapy â to reduce the frequency of bizarre, disturbing, and deviant behaviour
Group Therapy â promotes social interaction, sense of cohesiveness, identification, and insight

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 68-year-old man with recently diagnosed schizophrenia for the first time in his life, who is married, had been a well-functioning professional, and whose family history includes only mood disorders â has which prognostic profile?
options: Good prognosis â late onset, male sex (less favourable but offset by other factors), married marital status, good premorbid personality, and family history of mood disorder rather than schizophrenia are all good prognostic factors
Poor prognosis â male sex, family history of schizophrenia, and long duration are poor prognostic factors that override the other favourable features
Poor prognosis â late onset in males is associated with worse outcomes because the presentation is atypical and often misdiagnosed, delaying appropriate treatment

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A patient with schizophrenia is being discharged. The nurse provides advice on discharge. Which of the following is specifically stated in the text as discharge advice?
options: Patient is encouraged to take his prescribed medication; keep to his hospital appointment; avoid stress situations; ensure adequate rest and sleep; avoid the use of psychoactive substances such as cannabis
Patient is encouraged to avoid all social interactions for six months to prevent relapse; attend weekly psychiatric outpatient appointments; store all medications in a locked cabinet
Patient is encouraged to immediately resume all previous occupational roles; join a gym to channel energy; reduce all prescribed medications gradually over three months

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 55-year-old woman develops symptoms of PTSD after a serious motor vehicle accident. The nurse understands that in addition to direct trauma, individual susceptibility factors also influence PTSD development. Which factors are specifically named in the text?
options: Pre-existing mental health conditions such as anxiety or depression can make a person more vulnerable; genetic factors and history of childhood trauma or abuse may also contribute
Age at time of trauma, gender, educational level, and socioeconomic status are the primary individual susceptibility factors for PTSD development
The severity of the traumatic event and the number of previous traumatic exposures are the only individual susceptibility factors that predict PTSD development

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 70-year-old man develops rapid personality changes, starts using profane language, shows poor judgment, and has difficulty finding words. His memory is relatively spared. Which type of dementia is MOST consistent with this presentation?
options: Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD) â a group of dementias that primarily affect the frontal and temporal lobes, causing changes in behaviour, personality, language difficulties, and problems with executive functioning
Alzheimer's Disease â characterized by beta-amyloid plaques and tau tangles causing progressive loss of brain cells including areas controlling memory first
Vascular Dementia â occurs due to damage to blood vessels in the brain from stroke or small vessel disease, typically causing stepwise cognitive decline

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A patient with schizophrenia tells a nurse, 'When I read the newspaper, every article is about me and contains hidden messages for me.' The nurse correctly identifies this as which symptom?
options: Ideas of Reference â the patient thinks something in his surroundings is intended to have meaning for him when no such meaning is intended
Delusion of Grandeur â the patient states he has untold wealth or is a famous person of historic importance
DÃ©jÃ  vu â when a person feels undue familiarity to an event or person that is actually new to them

stem: Positive symptoms of schizophrenia are additional or excess experiences. The four positive symptoms listed are: ____________, ____________, thought disorders, and ____________.

Negative symptoms of schizophrenia involve reduction or absence of normal functions. The six negative symptoms listed are: Avolition, ____________, Blunt/Flattened Affect, ____________, Apathy, and ____________.
Eugene Bleuler maintained the 4 A's as distinctive and fundamental features of schizophrenia. They are: ____________, Autism, ____________, and Association loosening.
Bleuler's 'Autism' in schizophrenia means the patient is socially ____________, preferring to live in a world of ____________ rather than interact with the social world.
DÃ©jÃ  vu is when a person feels undue ____________ to an event or person, while Jamais vu is a feeling that a ____________ situation is ____________.

Catatonic schizophrenia involves disturbances in motor behaviour. The state where the patient assumes a strange posture for a long time is called catatonic ____________, also called ____________ flexibility.
The DSM-5 Duration criterion for schizophrenia requires continuous signs of illness for at least ____________ months, including at least ____________ month of active-phase symptoms.
Good prognostic factors of schizophrenia include: ____________ onset, female sex, ____________ marital status, short duration, family history of ____________ disorder.
Poor prognostic factors of schizophrenia include: insidious/____________ onset, ____________ sex, single/divorced/widowed status, long duration, and predominance of ____________ symptoms.
Typical antipsychotic drugs for schizophrenia include ____________, Haloperidol, ____________, and Trifluoperazine. Atypical antipsychotics include Risperidone, ____________, and Olanzepine.

Social Skills Training helps to address behaviours such as poor ____________ contact, odd facial ____________, and lack of ____________ in social situations through role play and homework assignments.

PTSD is characterized by a range of symptoms that can be categorized into four main clusters: ____________-experiencing symptoms, ____________ symptoms, Hyperarousal symptoms, and Cognitive and ____________ symptoms.

The three psychotherapy approaches named in the text for PTSD treatment are: Cognitive ____________ Therapy (CBT), Eye Movement ____________ and Reprocessing (EMDR), and ____________ Exposure Therapy (PE).

Medications for PTSD named in the text are Selective Serotonin ____________ Inhibitors (SSRIs) and Serotonin-____________ Reuptake Inhibitors (SNRIs), prescribed particularly when associated ____________ or depressive symptoms are present.

Dementia is a syndrome characterized by a ____________ and persistent decline in cognitive abilities; it is not a specific ____________ but rather a term used to describe a group of symptoms associated with a loss of ____________ function.

Alzheimer's disease is characterized by the accumulation of abnormal protein ____________ (beta-amyloid) and ____________ (tau) in the brain, leading to progressive loss of brain ____________ and cognitive decline.

Lewy Body Dementia is characterized by the presence of abnormal protein deposits called ____________ bodies; individuals may experience cognitive ____________, visual ____________, and Parkinson's-like symptoms such as tremors and stiffness.

Frontotemporal Dementia primarily affects the ____________ and ____________ lobes of the brain; it can cause changes in behaviour, ____________, language difficulties, and problems with executive functioning.
Paranoid Type schizophrenia is characterized by prominent ____________ and auditory ____________; individuals had ____________ or grandiose delusions.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A patient maintains a rigid, raised-arm posture for 90 minutes in the psychiatric ward. The nurse identifies this as catatonic ____________, also called ____________ flexibility, characteristic of ____________ schizophrenia.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A 70-year-old woman with schizophrenia is being assessed for prognosis. She has a family history of bipolar disorder, was previously well-functioning, and is married. The nurse identifies her prognosis as ____________ based on the presence of family history of ____________ disorder, good ____________ personality, and married status.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A 68-year-old man with dementia develops visual hallucinations of small animals, his alertness varies greatly during the day, and he has developed a tremor and shuffling gait. The nurse identifies this as ____________ Body Dementia (LBD), characterized by cognitive ____________ and ____________-like symptoms.
CASE SCENARIO: A patient with schizophrenia repeats every word and phrase the nurse says to him during the assessment. The nurse correctly identifies this as:
options: Echolalia â repeating others' words, listed as a symptom of catatonic schizophrenia alongside mutism, echopraxia, and waxy flexibility
Echopraxia â imitating others' movements automatically in catatonic schizophrenia
Verbigeration â monotonous repetition of one's own phrases, a stream of talk disturbance

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A PTSD patient tells the nurse he cannot go near the market where his accident occurred, avoids watching road traffic shows, and changes the subject whenever anyone talks about cars. Which PTSD symptom cluster is MOST dominant in his presentation?
options: Avoidance symptoms â the patient actively avoids places, people, activities, or situations that remind him of the traumatic event and tries to avoid discussing or thinking about it
Re-experiencing symptoms â the patient has intrusive memories, flashbacks, and nightmares related to the traumatic event
Hyperarousal symptoms â the patient is constantly on edge, has an exaggerated startle response, difficulty concentrating, irritability, and sleep disturbances

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A patient recently diagnosed with schizophrenia has the following profile: male, 18 years old, insidious onset, single, family history of schizophrenia in his father, and his presentation is predominantly negative symptoms. Which prognostic classification is MOST accurate?
options: Poor prognosis â early/insidious onset, male sex, single marital status, family history of schizophrenia (especially simple and undifferentiated subtype), and predominance of negative symptoms are all poor prognostic factors
Good prognosis â young age at onset means longer time for treatment to work; single status allows full focus on recovery without domestic responsibilities
Intermediate prognosis â the young age and early treatment initiation offset the negative prognostic indicators of male sex and predominance of negative symptoms

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A newly admitted schizophrenia patient refuses to shower, has matted hair, and smells of urine. The family reports he has been like this for months. Which nursing management action is MOST directly indicated?
options: Physical care â ensure maintenance of personal hygiene as part of the nursing management of schizophrenia
Psychological care â reassure the patient and his relative and involve them in his care to address the behavioural deterioration
Social Skills Training â help to address the patient's lack of spontaneity and poor self-care through role play and homework assignments

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 72-year-old woman with a 3-year history of progressive memory loss is found to have beta-amyloid plaques and tau tangles on brain imaging. Her family notes she initially forgot recent events, then names, and now cannot recognise them. Which dementia type is MOST consistent?
options: Alzheimer's disease â the most common form of dementia, characterized by accumulation of abnormal protein plaques (beta-amyloid) and tangles (tau) in the brain, leading to progressive loss of brain cells and cognitive decline
Vascular Dementia â occurring when there is damage to the blood vessels leading to reduced blood flow and oxygen supply from stroke, small vessel disease, or other vascular disorders
Lewy Body Dementia â characterized by Lewy bodies in the brain with cognitive fluctuations, visual hallucinations, and Parkinson's-like symptoms
